inputs.story: Waterford Spa had a full appointment calendar booked today. Help Janice figure out the schedule by matching each masseuse to her client, and determine the total price for each. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
2. Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
3. Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
4. Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.
5. Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.
inputs.constants:
Clients: Aimee; Ginger; Freda; Hannah.
Prices: $150; $160; $170; $180.
Masseuses: Lynda; Nancy; Teri; Whitney.
inputs.solution:
Clients	Prices	Masseuses
Aimee	$170	Nancy
Ginger	$150	Teri
Freda	$180	Whitney
Hannah	$160	Lynda

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
Waterford Spa had a full appointment calendar booked today. Help Janice figure out the schedule by matching each masseuse to her client, and determine the total price for each. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
2. Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
3. Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
4. Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.
5. Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.

Constants:
Clients: Aimee; Ginger; Freda; Hannah.
Prices: $150; $160; $170; $180.
Masseuses: Lynda; Nancy; Teri; Whitney.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Understand the Goal**: The user wants an ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that solves a specific logic grid puzzle. The program should be written in a format compatible with Clingo, following the provided examples and rules.

2.  **Analyze the Problem**:
   - **Story**: Waterford Spa appointment calendar. Match each masseuse to her client and determine the total price for each.
   - **Categories**:
     - Clients: Aimee; Ginger; Freda; Hannah.
     - Prices: $150; $160; $170; $180. (Strip $ → 150, 160, 170, 180)
     - Masseuses: Lynda; Nancy; Teri; Whitney.
   - **Clues**:
     1. Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
     2. Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
     3. Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
     4. Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.
     5. Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.
   - **Note**: The puzzle asks to match each *masseuse* to her *client* and determine the *price*. Wait, the clues mention "Hannah paid...", "Freda paid...", "Teri's client", "Lynda's client", "Nancy's client". This implies that the *clients* are the ones paying? Or maybe the names listed under "Clients" are actually the people paying, and the "Masseuses" are the staff. Let's check the constants:
     - Clients: Aimee, Ginger, Freda, Hannah.
     - Masseuses: Lynda, Nancy, Teri, Whitney.
     - Prices: 150, 160, 170, 180.
     - Clues refer to "Hannah paid", "Freda paid", "Teri's client", "Lynda's client", "Nancy's client". This fits: Clients pay money. Masseuses serve clients. Each masseuse serves exactly one client, and each client is served by exactly one masseuse. The price is associated with the client/masseuse pair.
     - Actually, in grid puzzles, usually each row/column represents a category. Here we have 3 categories: Clients, Prices, Masseuses. We need to map them together. Let's define a predicate `serve(Masseuse, Client, Price)`.
     - Wait, the clues say "Hannah paid more than Teri's client." This implies Hannah is a client, and Teri's client is another client. So `Client` is paid. `Masseuse` serves a `Client`. `Price` is what the client pays. So each `Masseuse` is linked to a `Client` and a `Price`.
     - Let's verify: 4 masseuses, 4 clients, 4 prices. Each masseuse serves exactly one client. The price is tied to the service/client. So `serve(M, C, P)` means masseuse M serves client C who paid P.
     - Clue 1: Hannah paid more than Teri's client. -> `P_h > P_teri_client` where `P_h` is price of client Hannah, `P_teri_client` is price of client served by Teri.
     - Clue 2: Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client. -> `P_f = P_lynda_client + 20`
     - Clue 3: Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client. -> `P_h = P_nancy_client - 10`
     - Clue 4: Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients. -> `C_nancy != "Hannah"`, `C_nancy != "Ginger"`, `"Hannah" != "Ginger"` (the last is trivial).
     - Clue 5: Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client. -> `{P=180; C="Lynda's client"? Wait, Lynda's client is a client. So Hannah is either the client with price 180, or Hannah is the client served by Lynda.} -> `{P=180; C="Lynda_client"? No, the clue says "Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client." This means either Hannah's price is 180, or Hannah is the client of Lynda. So `{P=180; C="Hannah"}`? Wait, if `serve(M, C, P)`, then for the tuple where `C="Hannah"`, either `P=180` or `M="Lynda"`. Let's rephrase: For the client Hannah, either her price is 180, or her masseuse is Lynda. So: `:- serve(M, "Hannah", P), P!=180, M!="Lynda".` Or using the pattern: `{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- serve(M, "Hannah", P).`

3.  **Map to ASP Structure**:
   - Constants:
     - `client("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").`
     - `price(150; 160; 170; 180).`
     - `masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").`
   - Predicate: `serve(Masseuse, Client, Price)`
   - Search space: `{serve(M, C, P): client(C), price(P)}=1 :- masseuse(M).`
   - Uniqueness: `{M1=M2; C1=C2; P1=P2}=0 :- serve(M1,C1,P1), serve(M2,C2,P2), (M1,C1,P1)!=(M2,C2,P2).`
   - Clues encoding:
     1. `Hannah paid more than Teri's client.`
        Find prices: `P_h` for `C="Hannah"`, `P_t` for `serve("Teri", C_t, P_t)`.
        Rule: `P1 > P2 :- serve(E1, C1, P1), serve(E2, C2, P2), C1="Hannah", E2="Teri".`
     2. `Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.`
        `P1 = P2 + 20 :- serve(E1, C1, P1), serve(E2, C2, P2), C1="Freda", E2="Lynda".`
     3. `Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.`
        `P1 = P2 - 10 :- serve(E1, C1, P1), serve(E2, C2, P2), C1="Hannah", E2="Nancy".`
     4. `Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.`
        Means `C_nancy != "Hannah"`, `C_nancy != "Ginger"`, `"Hannah" != "Ginger"` (trivial).
        Rules:
        `C1 != C2 :- serve(E1, C1, P1), serve(E2, C2, P2), E1="Nancy", C2="Hannah".`
        `C1 != C2 :- serve(E1, C1, P1), serve(E2, C2, P2), E1="Nancy", C2="Ginger".`
        (Alternatively, `:- serve(M, "Nancy_client", P), M="Nancy", C="Hannah".` Wait, `serve(M, C, P)` has `C` as client. So `:- serve("Nancy", "Hannah", _).` and `:- serve("Nancy", "Ginger", _).`)
        Let's stick to the inequality pattern or negative constraint.
        `:- serve("Nancy", "Hannah", _).`
        `:- serve("Nancy", "Ginger", _).`
     5. `Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.`
        Means for client Hannah: either `P=180` or `M="Lynda"`.
        Rule: `{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- serve(M, "Hannah", P).`

   Let's double check clue 4: "Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients."
   This implies the client served by Nancy is not Hannah, and not Ginger. Also Hannah != Ginger (given).
   So: `:- serve("Nancy", "Hannah", _).` `:- serve("Nancy", "Ginger", _).`
   This is simpler and matches the pattern `:- L1, ..., Ln.`

   Let's verify clue 5: "Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client."
   This means: In the row for client Hannah, either Price is 180, or Masseuse is Lynda.
   ASP: `{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- serve(M, "Hannah", P).`

   Check clue 1, 2, 3 syntax from examples:
   Example 1 uses: `P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".`
   I will use similar syntax.
   Clue 1: `P1 > P2 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), C1="Hannah", M2="Teri".`
   Clue 2: `P1 = P2 + 20 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), C1="Freda", M2="Lynda".`
   Clue 3: `P1 = P2 - 10 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), C1="Hannah", M2="Nancy".`

   Wait, in Clue 2: "Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client." -> `P_freda = P_lynda_client + 20`.
   In ASP: `P1 = P2 + 20 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), C1="Freda", M2="Lynda".` Correct.

   Let's check consistency of prices: 150, 160, 170, 180.
   From 1: P_hannah > P_teri_client.
   From 2: P_freda = P_lynda_client + 20.
   From 3: P_hannah = P_nancy_client - 10.
   Possible pairs for (Freda, Lynda's client): (170, 150), (180, 160).
   Possible pairs for (Hannah, Nancy's client): (150, 160), (160, 170), (170, 180).
   Since P_hannah > P_teri_client, P_hannah can't be 150. So P_hannah is 160 or 170.
   If P_hannah = 160, P_nancy_client = 170.
   If P_hannah = 170, P_nancy_client = 180.
   Clue 5: Hannah is either P=180 or M="Lynda". Since P_hannah is 160 or 170, it must be that M="Lynda" for Hannah. So Lynda serves Hannah.
   If Lynda serves Hannah, then P_lynda_client = P_hannah.
   From Clue 2: P_freda = P_hannah + 20.
   So if P_hannah=160, P_freda=180. Then P_nancy_client=170. P_teri_client must be <160, so 150. Clients: Hannah(160), Freda(180), Ginger(150), Nancy's client(170). This works.
   If P_hannah=170, P_freda=190 (not in list). So P_hannah must be 160.
   Solution: Lynda-Hannah-160, Nancy-Ginger-170, Teri-Aimee-150, Whitney-Freda-180? Wait, check clue 4: Nancy's client is not Hannah or Ginger. Here Nancy's client is Ginger. That violates clue 4.
   Let's re-evaluate.
   Clue 4: Nancy's client != Hannah, Nancy's client != Ginger.
   So Nancy's client must be Aimee or Freda.
   From Clue 3: P_hannah = P_nancy_client - 10.
   If P_nancy_client = Aimee, P_hannah = P_aimee - 10.
   If P_nancy_client = Freda, P_hannah = P_freda - 10.
   We know P_freda = P_lynda_client + 20.
   Let's list prices: 150, 160, 170, 180.
   Pairs with diff 20: (150,170), (160,180). So (Lynda's client, Freda) is (150,170) or (160,180).
   Pairs with diff 10: (150,160), (160,170), (170,180). So (Hannah, Nancy's client) is (150,160), (160,170), or (170,180).
   From Clue 5: Hannah is either P=180 or served by Lynda.
   If Hannah is served by Lynda, then P_hannah = P_lynda_client.
   Then P_freda = P_hannah + 20.
   And P_hannah = P_nancy_client - 10 => P_nancy_client = P_hannah + 10.
   So we have P_hannah, P_hannah+10 (Nancy's client), P_hannah+20 (Freda).
   Prices must be in {150, 160, 170, 180}.
   If P_hannah=150 -> Nancy=160, Freda=170. But Clue 1 says P_hannah > P_teri_client. 150 > P_teri? Impossible since min is 150.
   If P_hannah=160 -> Nancy=170, Freda=180. P_teri < 160 -> P_teri=150.
   So: Hannah=160, Nancy's client=170, Freda=180, Teri's client=150.
   Clients assigned to prices: Hannah(160), Nancy's client(170), Freda(180), Teri's client(150).
   Remaining client for 150 and 170: Aimee, Ginger.
   Clue 4: Nancy's client is not Hannah or Ginger. So Nancy's client (170) must be Aimee.
   Then Teri's client (150) must be Ginger.
   So: Aimee=170, Ginger=150, Hannah=160, Freda=180.
   Masseuses: Lynda serves Hannah (from Clue 5 deduction).
   Teri serves Ginger.
   Nancy serves Aimee.
   Whitney serves Freda.
   Check clues:
   1. Hannah(160) > Teri's client(Ginger, 150). True.
   2. Freda(180) = Lynda's client(Hannah, 160) + 20. True.
   3. Hannah(160) = Nancy's client(Aimee, 170) - 10. True.
   4. Nancy's client(Aimee), Hannah, Ginger are different. True.
   5. Hannah is either P=180 or Lynda's client. She is Lynda's client. True.
   All constraints satisfied. Unique solution found.

   Now, encode this into ASP properly.

   Constants:
   `client("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").`
   `price(150; 160; 170; 180).`
   `masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").`

   Predicate: `serve(Masseuse, Client, Price)`

   Search space:
   `{serve(M, C, P): client(C), price(P)}=1 :- masseuse(M).`

   Uniqueness:
   `{M1=M2; C1=C2; P1=P2}=0 :- serve(M1,C1,P1), serve(M2,C2,P2), (M1,C1,P1)!=(M2,C2,P2).`

   Clues:
   1. `P1 > P2 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), C1="Hannah", M2="Teri".`
   2. `P1 = P2 + 20 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), C1="Freda", M2="Lynda".`
   3. `P1 = P2 - 10 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), C1="Hannah", M2="Nancy".`
   4. `:- serve("Nancy", "Hannah", _).`
      `:- serve("Nancy", "Ginger", _).`
      (Note: using `_` or just omitting third arg in body is fine in Clingo, but I'll stick to full vars or `_` for clarity. Actually, Clingo allows `:- serve("Nancy", "Hannah", P).`)
   5. `{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- serve(M, "Hannah", P).`

   Check rule 5 format from examples: `{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.`
   My rule 5: `{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- serve(M, "Hannah", P).` -> This says for the row where client is Hannah, exactly one of P=180 or M="Lynda" holds. Correct.

   Let's verify Clue 4 encoding. The example uses inequality rules: `C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.`
   I can use:
   `C1 != C2 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), M1="Nancy", C2="Hannah".`
   `C1 != C2 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), M1="Nancy", C2="Ginger".`
   This is safer and matches the style.

   Let's assemble the program carefully.

   ```asp
   % Constants
   client("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
   price(150; 160; 170; 180).
   masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").

   % Search space: for each masseuse M, assign exactly one client C and price P.
   {serve(M, C, P): client(C), price(P)}=1 :- masseuse(M).

   % No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
   {M1=M2; C1=C2; P1=P2}=0 :- serve(M1,C1,P1), serve(M2,C2,P2), (M1,C1,P1)!=(M2,C2,P2).

   % 1. Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
   P1 > P2 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), C1="Hannah", M2="Teri".

   % 2. Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
   P1 = P2 + 20 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), C1="Freda", M2="Lynda".

   % 3. Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
   P1 = P2 - 10 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), C1="Hannah", M2="Nancy".

   % 4. Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.
   C1 != C2 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), M1="Nancy", C2="Hannah".
   C1 != C2 :- serve(M1, C1, P1), serve(M2, C2, P2), M1="Nancy", C2="Ginger".

   % 5. Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.
   {P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- serve(M, "Hannah", P).

   #show serve/3.
   ```

   Check against rules:
   - Constant declarations: `client("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").` etc. Matches rule 1.
   - Predicates: `serve/3` used. Matches rule 2.
   - Search space: `{serve(M, C, P): client(C), price(P)}=1 :- masseuse(M).` Matches rule 3.

% Constants
client("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
price(150; 160; 170; 180).
masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").

% Search space: for each masseuse M, assign exactly one client C and price P.
{schedule(M, C, P): client(C), price(P)}=1 :- masseuse(M).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{M1=M2; C1=C2; P1=P2}=0 :- schedule(M1,C1,P1), schedule(M2,C2,P2), (M1,C1,P1)!=(M2,C2,P2).

% 1. Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
P1 > P2 :- schedule(M1, C1, P1), schedule(M2, C2, P2), C1="Hannah", M2="Teri".

% 2. Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
P1 = P2 + 20 :- schedule(M1, C1, P1), schedule(M2, C2, P2), C1="Freda", M2="Lynda".

% 3. Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
P1 = P2 - 10 :- schedule(M1, C1, P1), schedule(M2, C2, P2), C1="Hannah", M2="Nancy".

% 4. Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.
C1 != C2 :- schedule(M1, C1, P1), schedule(M2, C2, P2), M1="Nancy", C2="Hannah".
C1 != C2 :- schedule(M1, C1, P1), schedule(M2, C2, P2), M1="Nancy", C2="Ginger".

% 5. Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.
{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- schedule(M, "Hannah", P).

#show schedule/3.

masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").
